Transported Hessian Adaptive-Threshold Calibration

  • Purpose
  • Retention-Residual Frontier
  • Best Feasible Settings
  • Quantile-Gate Heatmap
  • Robust-\(z\) Gate Heatmap
  • Degeneracy And Drop Mechanisms
  • Results Summary
    • What We Learned
  • Appendix
    • Run Metadata
    • Policy Grid
    • Summary Table
    • Reproducibility

Purpose

The transported graph Hessian creates a row by comparing a source directional difference at \(u\) with a transported directional difference at \(u'\). For a candidate set at the target fiber, let

\[ s_{(1)} \le s_{(2)} \le \cdots \le s_{(J)} \]

be the ordered match scores. Adaptive thresholding asks whether the best match is locally good and locally separated enough to trust:

\[ m=s_{(2)}-s_{(1)}. \]

The previous report showed the main danger: adaptive rules can reduce polynomial residuals by dropping too many rows. This calibration report therefore treats row retention as a constraint, not as a secondary detail. The main question is:

\[ \text{minimize } R_{\mathrm{linear}} = \frac{\|AP_{\mathrm{linear}}\|_F}{\|P_{\mathrm{linear}}\|_F} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \rho_{\mathrm{row}}\ge 0.50 \text{ and nondegeneracy}. \]

Build: 2026-05-18 20:55:37 EDT
Mode: full
Backends: cmdscale
Local grip requested: TRUE
True edge-KK available: TRUE
Edge-KK function: grip.optimize.edge.kk.layout
Policies tested: 42

Retention-Residual Frontier

Each point is one threshold setting on one graph. The vertical dashed line is the minimum retention constraint \(\rho_{\mathrm{row}}\ge 0.50\). Points to the left of the line are informative as diagnostics, but they are not acceptable default candidates because the operator may have been hollowed out.

The frontier is the main calibration view. A threshold family is promising only when it improves linear residuals while staying to the right of the retention line. Points with low residual and very low retention are not wins; they are operator-degeneracy warnings.

Best Feasible Settings

The next figure shows the best feasible setting for each graph/backend pair under the \(0.50\) row-retention constraint. The baseline fixed angle/length rule is included, so an adaptive rule must beat a conservative fixed gate while retaining enough rows.

This view answers the practical default question. If the same adaptive family wins repeatedly across graph types, it is a plausible default. If the fixed baseline keeps winning, adaptive gates should remain diagnostic or case-specific until better calibration is available.

Quantile-Gate Heatmap

The local quantile rule accepts rows when

\[ q_s=\widehat F_s(s_{(1)})\le q_{\max}, \qquad q_m=\widehat F_{\Delta s}(m)\ge q_{\min}. \]

The heatmap reports the best feasible linear residual at each \((q_{\max},q_{\min})\), minimizing over the optional entropy-fraction gate. Blank cells did not meet the row-retention constraint.

The quantile rule is intentionally local-degree aware, but the heatmap makes its danger visible: as \(q_{\min}\) becomes too strict, many graphs lose too many rows and disappear from the feasible set.

Robust-\(z\) Gate Heatmap

The robust-\(z\) rule accepts rows when

\[ z_s = \frac{\operatorname{median}(s)-s_{(1)}}{\operatorname{scale}(s)} \ge z_{s,\min}, \qquad z_m = \frac{m-\operatorname{median}(\Delta s)}{\operatorname{scale}(\Delta s)} \ge z_{m,\min}. \]

The heatmap reports the best feasible residual at each \((z_{s,\min},z_{m,\min})\), again minimizing over the optional entropy-fraction gate.

The robust-\(z\) rule is less tied to the discreteness of empirical quantiles, but it still needs calibration: large \(z\)-thresholds can reject exactly the path and boundary comparisons needed to keep the operator meaningful.

Degeneracy And Drop Mechanisms

Degeneracy is not a secondary warning; it is the failure mode that this report is designed to expose. A very low residual with very low row retention means the operator stopped testing the polynomial probe.

These figures distinguish a useful confidence rule from a destructive thinning rule. A good rule should mostly remove ambiguous local matches while keeping enough rows to maintain a low-dimensional null space.

82 path_1d cmdscale local_quantile quantile_qs0.50_qm0.25_ef0.50 0.8810 0.0000 2 0.5000
11 sampled_sknn_2d_n24 cmdscale baseline baseline_fixed_geometric 0.6514 4.1374 1 0.1666
14 sampled_sknn_2d_n32 cmdscale baseline baseline_fixed_geometric 0.6358 5.9318 1 0.1369

The calibration experiment should be read conservatively. The best threshold is not the one with the smallest residual in isolation; it is the one with the smallest residual among policies that retain enough rows and avoid degeneracy. This report therefore turns adaptive thresholding from an attractive idea into a measurable tradeoff.

What We Learned

Under the 50% row-retention constraint, the best broadly feasible policy is baseline_fixed_geometric from the baseline family. Its median feasible linear residual is 0.539 and its median row retention is 0.651.

Best feasible policy counts by graph/backend pair: baseline: 4; local_quantile: 2; local_robust_z: 1.

The practical conclusion is that adaptive gates are useful calibration tools, but they are not yet a safe universal default. The fixed angle/length gate remains the conservative baseline whenever an adaptive rule improves residuals mainly by moving leftward on the retention frontier.

The expected outcome is not necessarily a single universal default. It may be that fixed angle/length gates are safer on irregular or sampled graphs, while adaptive gates are useful on clean path/grid references. If the frontier shows that pattern, the next implementation step should expose adaptive gates as diagnostics or case-specific policies rather than making them the default.

Reproducibility

Smoke render:

Rscript dev/graph-trend-filtering/render_transported_hessian_adaptive_threshold_sweep.R

Full render:

GFLOW_ADAPTIVE_THRESHOLD_SWEEP_MODE=full Rscript dev/graph-trend-filtering/render_transported_hessian_adaptive_threshold_sweep.R
